title: Chapter 24: The Glitch Garden
content: Wren couldn't sleep. Every time she closed them, she saw the room—white walls, IV stand, camera in the corner. Ash strapped to a chair while a woman with a clipboard narrated his destruction.

She got up. Found her pack. Dug past the spare clothes and the water filter to the bottom, where she kept what mattered: three stubs of charcoal, a stick of red chalk, and a can of spray paint she'd taken from a construction site two weeks ago.
Art supplies. The most valuable things she owned.

She walked until the tunnels opened into a chamber she'd noticed before but never entered. A former reservoir—circular, domed, the ceiling thick with decades of condensation. Water had dried to mineral tracks on the walls, leaving pale cascading patterns like frozen waterfalls.
The acoustics were strange. Her footsteps returned to her from three directions at once.
Good. Private.
She chose a wall and began.

The charcoal moved first. Broad strokes—a figure standing alone, rendered in the negative: the shape of a person defined by the space around them. Crowds on either side, dense and detailed, and in the center, nothing. An absence.
She didn't plan it. Her hand knew where to go.

The absence became Ash. Not his face—she didn't know his real face, the one that had belonged to Alexander Chen. She drew what she knew: the slope of his shoulders, the way he held his head when he was thinking, the curves of his tattoos flowing down his arms like rivers on a map.
Then she drew the others.
Mira, Stage 2, who wandered the tunnels talking to people who weren't there. Wren sketched her with a halo of whisper-thin lines, words made visible.
Kade and June, the siblings, drawn leaning against each other—their twin erasures so complete that even other invisibles sometimes forgot they were there.
Old Boz, Stage 1, still partially visible, his face half-rendered like a painting left unfinished.
She drew them all. Every invisible she'd met. Every ghost the city had swallowed.
Hours passed. The charcoal wore down to a nub. She switched to the red chalk and began adding color—accents, highlights, the warmth of bodies that the world above refused to acknowledge.
The domed chamber transformed. What had been a blank concrete shell became a gallery. A record. A wall of witnesses who could not be seen but refused to vanish entirely.
Because here they were. Drawn in charcoal and chalk and the desperate pressure of a hand that needed to make marks.
Wren stepped back. Her fingers were black and red. Her arms ached. The wall in front of her breathed with faces.
She hadn't thought about sleep. She hadn't thought about Ash's story. She'd just—made. And for the first time in weeks, the knot in her chest had loosened.
Not gone. Just... looser.
Footsteps echoed from the entrance.
Wren tensed, but it was only Boz, shuffling in with his uneven gait. His Stage 1 gave him a shimmer at the edges—visible enough to recognize, invisible enough to startle.
"Huh," he said, examining the wall. "That's something."
"Don't look yet. It's not finished."
"When's it going to be finished?"
Wren considered. "Never, probably."
Boz grunted. "Good. Unfinished things stay alive longer." He ran a thick finger along the edge of Mira's portrait, careful not to smudge. "You got her. The way she talks to the air. You got it."
"She's not talking to the air. She's talking to who she used to be."
"Same thing, isn't it?"
By the next evening, word had spread.
Invisibles came singly or in pairs, stood in the reservoir chamber, and studied the wall. Nobody touched it without asking Wren first. An unspoken rule, established without discussion.
Then Kade brought a pen. June brought wire and beads. Boz contributed a broken watch whose hands had stopped at different times—11:47 and 3:22—and hung it near Ash's portrait without explanation.

Mira, of all people, brought a flower. A real one—somehow sourced from the city above, still half-alive, yellow petals bright against the grey concrete. She wedged it into a crack in the wall beneath her own portrait.
"Don't know why," she said. "Seemed right."

Others followed. Scraps of fabric, tokens, messages scratched into the concrete with rocks. The chamber became something it had never been: a place that belonged to people. People the world above refused to see.
Wren stood in the center, turning slowly, taking it in. The faces, the objects, the accumulated proof of existence.
She thought: *If they erased us, at least this will prove we were here.*
She thought: *This is a garden. A garden of glitches.*
The third day, Silas appeared.

He was one of the quiet ones—older, maybe sixty, with a wispy beard and hands that shook. Stage 2. He'd been invisible for eight years, longer than almost anyone else in the Underlayer. He survived on routine and silence, speaking rarely, contributing what he could without drawing attention.
Wren had drawn him two days ago. A simple portrait—his face in profile, the angles of his features sharp against the dark wall, his beard a blur of charcoal.
He stood in front of it for a long time. Wren watched from across the chamber, uncertain whether to approach.
When he finally spoke, his voice cracked from disuse.
"My daughter draws like that."
Wren froze.

Silas didn't turn. Didn't elaborate. Just stared at the portrait with an expression she couldn't read—something between recognition and grief, as if the charcoal lines had opened a door he'd sealed shut years ago.
"Like what?" she asked carefully.

"Like she's trying to remember something she's never seen." He touched the edge of his own portrait, fingertip grazing the charcoal beard. "Loose. Searching. The lines don't stay where you put them. They wander."
Wren's pulse quickened. "Your daughter—does she draw professionally?"
"She did. Before." Silas dropped his hand. "I don't know what she does now. I haven't spoken to her in eight years."
He walked away before she could ask more.

Wren stood alone in what the invisibles had started calling the Glitch Garden. The name had spread the same way everything did down here—whisper, rumor, a phrase that stuck because it was true. They were glitches. This was their garden.
She looked at Silas's portrait. Then at her own hands, still stained with charcoal.
His daughter. A daughter who drew like her. Loose, searching lines that wandered.
It was probably nothing. A coincidence. An old man seeing echoes in the dark.
But something about it snagged in her mind like a thread on a nail. She couldn't leave it alone.
She found a blank section of wall and began a new drawing. Not a portrait this time. A door—half-open, light spilling through, the space beyond unfinished.
A door she hadn't walked through yet.
Behind her, the Glitch Garden hummed with its quiet evidence. Paintings and tokens and the accumulated weight of people who refused to be nothing, even if the world insisted otherwise.
Wren drew until her fingers ached.
Then she drew some more.
